critic_raw: Three issues: preprocessing leakage, multi-confounded comparison preventing attribution, and temporal split violation.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers fit on entire dataset before splitting — data leakage
Multi-modal model fine-tunes ResNet-50 while baseline uses TF-IDF + traditional classifiers — three simultaneous advantages prevent attribution
Stratified random split on 24-month clinical data ignores temporal ordering — temporal leakage

round_transcripts.critic: Three issues: preprocessing leakage, multi-confounded comparison preventing attribution, and temporal split violation.
round_transcripts.defender:
**On Issue 1 (Preprocessing leakage):** Confirmed by explicit design text.

**On Issue 2 (Multi-confounded comparison):** The multi-modal model is fine-tuned with deep learning across all modalities; the baseline is text-only TF-IDF + traditional classifier. The hypothesis is 'does multi-modal data improve diagnostic accuracy?' — framed as a data modality question. However, the design tests architecture simultaneously with modality, preventing attribution. A fair ablation would include text-only deep learning (same architecture, no images) to isolate the modality contribution. This issue is valid as a scope-of-inference limitation.

**On Issue 3 (Temporal split):** Confirmed — stratified random split on longitudinal clinical data violates temporal causal ordering.

round_transcripts.critic: All three issues confirmed. Issue 1 (preprocessing leakage) is the most fundamental. Issue 2 limits interpretation. Issue 3 enables temporal leakage.
round_transcripts.defender: All three issues stand. Issues 1 and 3 are fundamental methodological flaws. Issue 2 is an interpretability limitation that prevents causal attribution of any observed result.

all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing transformers fit on entire dataset before splitting — data leakage
Multi-modal model fine-tunes ResNet-50 while baseline uses TF-IDF + traditional — three simultaneous advantages prevent attribution
Stratified random split on 24-month clinical data ignores temporal ordering
